topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the room had become a physical weight. Every rustle of paper, every soft turning of a page, sent a jolt of icy dread through my system. I kept catching my roommate, Liam, engrossed in the worn leather-bound book I’d foolishly left open on my desk. My breath hitched in my throat. It was my journal—the one filled with the messy, raw confessions of my anxieties and dreams. A slow, cold burn started in my chest. I watched his eyes flick up, momentarily catching mine, a flicker of something unreadable crossing his face before he quickly looked back down. My hands, resting on my textbooks, started to tremble uncontrollably. I needed to move, to slam the book shut, but my feet felt cemented to the floor. The air in the small apartment felt thin, suffocating.

[story 2]

It started subtly: a misplaced bookmark, a page slightly askew. Then, one Tuesday afternoon, I walked in to find my private thoughts laid out in neat, annotated script across the open pages of my diary. My stomach plummeted. I stood frozen in the doorway, the sound of my own ragged breathing deafening in the quiet room. I didn't speak. I just stared, my jaw tight, the blood draining from my face until I felt strangely numb. Liam was sitting at his desk, ostensibly studying, but his gaze kept darting toward the journal. When he saw me, his composure cracked; he mumbled something about needing to check something, his voice tight and strained. The sheer violation of my space made my skin crawl, and I felt a desperate, frantic urge to flee the room entirely.

[story 3]

I found the evidence tucked beneath a stack of graphic novels: my journal, its pages deliberately turned to a specific entry I hadn't even thought about sharing. The realization hit me like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. My hands began to shake so violently I had to grip the edge of my desk to keep from dropping them. Every nerve ending in my body was screaming a warning. I waited, rigid, for him to appear. When he did, he offered a weak, apologetic smile that didn't reach his eyes. "Just curious," he said, his tone overly casual, which only made the situation feel infinitely worse. I couldn't form a coherent sentence. I just stared at the open book, the world narrowing down to the scratch of his pen and the horrifying knowledge that my deepest vulnerabilities were now his secret. A wave of nausea washed over me, leaving me shaky and utterly exposed.
